text:
Our knowledge on the impact of populist governments on the European Union’s decision-making processes has remained relatively unknown. Using the crucial case method of refugee relocation and analyses of the unpolitics of European and national level governance, this article adds to the growing literature examining populist governments’ policy-making and actions at the EU level. Initial research demonstrated unpolitics where populist politicians disregard formal and informal rules and negotiating practices, and instead exploit and manufacture crises for political and social benefit. However, it remains unclear whether the refugee crisis is a unique policy area that is easily monopolised by populist Eurosceptic views or if unpolitics constitutes a systemic change.

This thematic issue explores the behaviour of populist governments in Council decision-making across policy areas, and what this tells us about the conditions under which unpolitics is manifest. We take unpolitics to be a vote-seeking strategy that is intended to boost the authority of a leader among voters in their home country. Our analysis suggests that unpolitics is most manifest in those policy areas which are high in gain but low in risk, and which can be framed and told as a simple tale that can be dramatized in the public arena of the Council of Ministers. As a vote-seeking strategy, unpolitics is a two-level game which is conditional upon domestic politics and socio-economic context. So far, European integration institutions have proved to be robust to unpolitics, but there is a danger that if it is practiced on a sufficiently consistent basis it will start to erode the prevailing norms and vocabulary of the European project.
